instruction: Design a Rust module organization strategy for a large application, showing how to use visibility modifiers, re-exports, and the module tree to create a clean public API.
output:
<think>
Rust's module system is powerful but can be confusing. The key principles are: modules control visibility, `pub` exposes items, and re-exports (`pub use`) create a clean facade.

For a large application, I need to think about:
1. Crate-level organization — what goes in lib.rs vs binary crates
2. Module hierarchy — grouping related types and functions
3. Visibility levels — `pub`, `pub(crate)`, `pub(super)`, private
4. Re-exports — creating a clean public API from internal module structure
5. Prelude pattern — convenience re-exports for common types

Best practices:
- Internal modules should be `pub(crate)` not `pub` — prevents leaking internals
- Use `mod.rs` or `module_name.rs` with `module_name/` directory
- Re-export commonly used types at the crate root
- Keep the module tree shallow (2-3 levels max)
- Group by domain, not by type (domain/user.rs not models/user.rs + services/user.rs)

I'll show a complete crate structure for a web service with domain, infrastructure, and API layers.
</think>

```rust
// === Crate structure ===
// src/
//   lib.rs          -- Crate root, re-exports, prelude
//   domain/
//     mod.rs        -- Domain module root
//     user.rs       -- User entity + repository trait
//     order.rs      -- Order aggregate
//     error.rs      -- Domain errors
//   infra/
//     mod.rs        -- Infrastructure module root  
//     db.rs         -- Database implementation
//     cache.rs      -- Cache implementation
//   api/
//     mod.rs        -- API layer
//     handlers.rs   -- HTTP handlers
//     dto.rs        -- Request/Response types

// ============================================================
// src/lib.rs — Clean public API via re-exports
// ============================================================

pub mod domain;
pub mod api;

// Infrastructure is internal — only exposed through domain traits
pub(crate) mod infra;

// Prelude for convenient imports
pub mod prelude {
    pub use crate::domain::{User, UserId, Order, OrderId};
    pub use crate::domain::error::DomainError;
    pub use crate::domain::UserRepository;
}

// ============================================================
// src/domain/mod.rs — Domain layer, pure business logic
// ============================================================

mod user;    // Private module
mod order;   // Private module
pub mod error;

// Re-export public types — users of this crate see domain::User,
// not domain::user::User
pub use user::{User, UserId, UserRepository, UserStatus};
pub use order::{Order, OrderId, OrderItem, OrderStatus};

// ============================================================
// src/domain/user.rs — User entity
// ============================================================

use super::error::DomainError;
use std::fmt;

/// Strongly-typed ID prevents mixing up user IDs with order IDs.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub struct UserId(uuid::Uuid);

impl UserId {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        Self(uuid::Uuid::new_v4())
    }
    
    pub fn from_uuid(id: uuid::Uuid) -> Self {
        Self(id)
    }
}

impl fmt::Display for UserId {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        write!(f, "{}", self.0)
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
pub enum UserStatus {
    Active,
    Suspended,
    Deleted,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct User {
    id: UserId,
    email: String,
    name: String,
    status: UserStatus,
    // Private field — only accessible within domain module
    pub(super) password_hash: String,
}

impl User {
    pub fn new(email: String, name: String, password_hash: String) -> Result<Self, DomainError> {
        if !email.contains('@') {
            return Err(DomainError::Validation("Invalid email".into()));
        }
        if name.is_empty() {
            return Err(DomainError::Validation("Name required".into()));
        }
        Ok(Self {
            id: UserId::new(),
            email,
            name,
            status: UserStatus::Active,
            password_hash,
        })
    }

    pub fn id(&self) -> UserId { self.id }
    pub fn email(&self) -> &str { &self.email }
    pub fn name(&self) -> &str { &self.name }
    pub fn status(&self) -> &UserStatus { &self.status }

    pub fn suspend(&mut self) -> Result<(), DomainError> {
        match self.status {
            UserStatus::Active => {
                self.status = UserStatus::Suspended;
                Ok(())
            }
            _ => Err(DomainError::InvalidState(
                format!("Cannot suspend user in {:?} state", self.status)
            )),
        }
    }
}

/// Repository trait — defined in domain, implemented in infra.
/// This is the dependency inversion boundary.
pub trait UserRepository: Send + Sync {
    fn save(&self, user: &User) -> Result<(), DomainError>;
    fn find_by_id(&self, id: UserId) -> Result<Option<User>, DomainError>;
    fn find_by_email(&self, email: &str) -> Result<Option<User>, DomainError>;
    fn delete(&self, id: UserId) -> Result<(), DomainError>;
}

// ============================================================
// src/domain/error.rs — Domain errors
// ============================================================

use thiserror::Error;

#[derive(Debug, Error)]
pub enum DomainError {
    #[error("Validation error: {0}")]
    Validation(String),
    
    #[error("Not found: {0}")]
    NotFound(String),
    
    #[error("Invalid state: {0}")]
    InvalidState(String),
    
    #[error("Infrastructure error: {0}")]
    Infrastructure(String),
}

// ============================================================
// src/infra/db.rs — Database implementation (pub(crate) only)
// ============================================================

use crate::domain::{User, UserId, UserRepository};
use crate::domain::error::DomainError;

/// Only visible within the crate — external users use the trait.
pub(crate) struct PostgresUserRepository {
    // pool: sqlx::PgPool,
}

impl PostgresUserRepository {
    pub(crate) fn new(/* pool: sqlx::PgPool */) -> Self {
        Self { /* pool */ }
    }
}

impl UserRepository for PostgresUserRepository {
    fn save(&self, user: &User) -> Result<(), DomainError> {
        // sqlx query here
        Ok(())
    }
    
    fn find_by_id(&self, id: UserId) -> Result<Option<User>, DomainError> {
        // sqlx query here
        Ok(None)
    }
    
    fn find_by_email(&self, email: &str) -> Result<Option<User>, DomainError> {
        Ok(None)
    }
    
    fn delete(&self, id: UserId) -> Result<(), DomainError> {
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

Key patterns: strongly-typed IDs prevent mixing `UserId` with `OrderId` at compile time. `pub(crate)` on infrastructure types prevents external code from depending on implementation details. Re-exports in `domain/mod.rs` create a flat namespace (`domain::User` not `domain::user::User`). The prelude module provides one-line imports for common types. Repository traits in the domain layer enforce dependency inversion — the infrastructure implements domain-defined interfaces.

instruction: Implement Rust error propagation design with custom error types, the `thiserror` pattern, and conversion between error types across module boundaries.
output:
<think>
Error handling in Rust is one of its strongest features, but designing error types for a real application requires thought. The key decisions:

1. When to use enums vs structs for errors
2. How to propagate errors across module boundaries using `From` conversions
3. When to use `thiserror` (library errors) vs `anyhow` (application errors)
4. How to preserve error context without losing the original error
5. Error type granularity — one big enum vs per-module errors

Best practices:
- Library code: use `thiserror` with specific error enums. Consumers can match on variants.
- Application code: use `anyhow` at the top level for ergonomic error handling.
- Each module/layer should have its own error type.
- Use `From` implementations to convert between layer errors.
- Include context: what operation failed, not just what went wrong.

The pattern I want to show: domain errors, infrastructure errors that convert to domain errors, and API errors that convert from domain errors to HTTP responses. Each layer maps errors at its boundary.
</think>

```rust
// ============================================================
// Domain layer errors — the innermost error type
// ============================================================

use std::fmt;
use thiserror::Error;

#[derive(Debug, Error)]
pub enum DomainError {
    #[error("Validation failed: {field} — {message}")]
    Validation { field: String, message: String },

    #[error("{entity} not found: {id}")]
    NotFound { entity: String, id: String },

    #[error("Operation not allowed: {0}")]
    Forbidden(String),

    #[error("Conflict: {0}")]
    Conflict(String),

    #[error("Internal error: {0}")]
    Internal(String),
}

impl DomainError {
    pub fn validation(field: impl Into<String>, msg: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        Self::Validation { field: field.into(), message: msg.into() }
    }

    pub fn not_found(entity: impl Into<String>, id: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        Self::NotFound { entity: entity.into(), id: id.into() }
    }
}

// ============================================================
// Infrastructure layer errors — convert FROM external errors
// ============================================================

#[derive(Debug, Error)]
pub enum InfraError {
    #[error("Database error: {0}")]
    Database(String),

    #[error("Cache error: {0}")]
    Cache(String),

    #[error("External service error: {service} — {message}")]
    ExternalService { service: String, message: String },

    #[error("Serialization error: {0}")]
    Serialization(String),

    #[error("Connection pool exhausted")]
    PoolExhausted,
}

// Convert external library errors to InfraError
// In real code, these would be for sqlx, redis, reqwest, etc.
impl From<std::io::Error> for InfraError {
    fn from(e: std::io::Error) -> Self {
        InfraError::Database(e.to_string())
    }
}

impl From<serde_json::Error> for InfraError {
    fn from(e: serde_json::Error) -> Self {
        InfraError::Serialization(e.to_string())
    }
}

// ============================================================
// KEY: Convert InfraError → DomainError at the boundary
// This is where dependency inversion happens for errors
// ============================================================

impl From<InfraError> for DomainError {
    fn from(e: InfraError) -> Self {
        match e {
            InfraError::PoolExhausted => {
                DomainError::Internal("Service temporarily unavailable".into())
            }
            other => DomainError::Internal(other.to_string()),
        }
    }
}

// ============================================================
// API layer errors — convert FROM DomainError to HTTP responses
// ============================================================

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct ApiError {
    pub status_code: u16,
    pub error_code: String,
    pub message: String,
}

impl From<DomainError> for ApiError {
    fn from(e: DomainError) -> Self {
        match e {
            DomainError::Validation { field, message } => ApiError {
                status_code: 400,
                error_code: "VALIDATION_ERROR".into(),
                message: format!("{}: {}", field, message),
            },
            DomainError::NotFound { entity, id } => ApiError {
                status_code: 404,
                error_code: "NOT_FOUND".into(),
                message: format!("{} {} not found", entity, id),
            },
            DomainError::Forbidden(msg) => ApiError {
                status_code: 403,
                error_code: "FORBIDDEN".into(),
                message: msg,
            },
            DomainError::Conflict(msg) => ApiError {
                status_code: 409,
                error_code: "CONFLICT".into(),
                message: msg,
            },
            DomainError::Internal(msg) => ApiError {
                status_code: 500,
                error_code: "INTERNAL_ERROR".into(),
                message: "An internal error occurred".into(), // Don't leak details
            },
        }
    }
}

impl fmt::Display for ApiError {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        write!(f, "[{}] {}: {}", self.status_code, self.error_code, self.message)
    }
}

// ============================================================
// Context extension trait — add context to errors ergonomically
// ============================================================

pub trait ErrorContext<T> {
    fn context(self, msg: impl Into<String>) -> Result<T, DomainError>;
    fn with_context(self, f: impl FnOnce() -> String) -> Result<T, DomainError>;
}

impl<T, E: Into<DomainError>> ErrorContext<T> for Result<T, E> {
    fn context(self, msg: impl Into<String>) -> Result<T, DomainError> {
        self.map_err(|e| {
            let domain_err = e.into();
            DomainError::Internal(format!("{}: {}", msg.into(), domain_err))
        })
    }

    fn with_context(self, f: impl FnOnce() -> String) -> Result<T, DomainError> {
        self.map_err(|e| {
            let domain_err = e.into();
            DomainError::Internal(format!("{}: {}", f(), domain_err))
        })
    }
}

// ============================================================
// Usage in a repository implementation
// ============================================================

pub struct UserService {
    // repo: Box<dyn UserRepository>,
}

impl UserService {
    /// Errors propagate cleanly across boundaries:
    /// sqlx::Error -> InfraError -> DomainError -> ApiError
    pub fn find_user(&self, user_id: &str) -> Result<String, DomainError> {
        // Infrastructure call — InfraError auto-converts to DomainError via `?`
        let raw_data = self.query_database(user_id)?;
        
        // Domain validation
        if raw_data.is_empty() {
            return Err(DomainError::not_found("User", user_id));
        }
        
        Ok(raw_data)
    }
    
    fn query_database(&self, id: &str) -> Result<String, InfraError> {
        // Simulated database call
        if id == "error" {
            Err(InfraError::Database("connection refused".into()))
        } else {
            Ok(format!("user_{}", id))
        }
    }
}

// In the API handler:
fn handle_request(user_id: &str) -> Result<String, ApiError> {
    let service = UserService {};
    // DomainError auto-converts to ApiError via `?`
    let user = service.find_user(user_id)?;
    Ok(format!("Found: {}", user))
}
```

The error propagation chain is: `sqlx::Error` -> `InfraError` (via `From`) -> `DomainError` (via `From`) -> `ApiError` (via `From`). Each layer maps errors at its boundary, translating implementation details into domain concepts. The API layer never sees `sqlx::Error` — it only sees `DomainError` variants that map cleanly to HTTP status codes. The `ErrorContext` trait adds `anyhow`-style context chaining without pulling in the `anyhow` dependency.

instruction: Implement the builder pattern in Rust with compile-time guarantees using typestate, ensuring required fields are set before building.
output:
<think>
The builder pattern in Rust can be implemented in two ways: runtime validation (check at build time, return Result) or compile-time validation using typestate (illegal states are unrepresentable).

Typestate uses phantom types to encode the builder's state in the type system. Each required field transitions the builder from "not set" to "set" state. The `build()` method is only available when ALL required fields are in the "set" state.

This is powerful because it catches missing-field bugs at compile time, not runtime. But it requires some boilerplate.

Design decisions:
1. Use zero-sized types as type parameters to represent state
2. Each setter method returns a new builder type with updated state
3. `build()` is only implemented for the fully-configured state
4. Optional fields don't affect the typestate
5. The builder consumes itself on each setter (move semantics)

I'll implement both approaches — typestate for compile-time safety and a simpler runtime-checked builder for comparison.
</think>

```rust
use std::marker::PhantomData;

// ============================================================
// Typestate markers — zero-sized types encoding builder state
// ============================================================

pub struct NotSet;
pub struct Set;

// ============================================================
// The target struct
// ============================================================

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct ServerConfig {
    host: String,
    port: u16,
    max_connections: usize,
    // Optional fields
    tls_cert: Option<String>,
    read_timeout_ms: u64,
    log_level: String,
}

// ============================================================
// Typestate Builder — compile-time field validation
// ============================================================

pub struct ServerConfigBuilder<HostState, PortState, MaxConnState> {
    host: Option<String>,
    port: Option<u16>,
    max_connections: Option<usize>,
    tls_cert: Option<String>,
    read_timeout_ms: u64,
    log_level: String,
    // PhantomData carries type state without runtime cost
    _host: PhantomData<HostState>,
    _port: PhantomData<PortState>,
    _max_conn: PhantomData<MaxConnState>,
}

impl ServerConfig {
    /// Entry point — all required fields start as NotSet.
    pub fn builder() -> ServerConfigBuilder<NotSet, NotSet, NotSet> {
        ServerConfigBuilder {
            host: None,
            port: None,
            max_connections: None,
            tls_cert: None,
            read_timeout_ms: 30_000,
            log_level: "info".to_string(),
            _host: PhantomData,
            _port: PhantomData,
            _max_conn: PhantomData,
        }
    }
}

// Setting `host` transitions HostState from NotSet to Set
impl<P, M> ServerConfigBuilder<NotSet, P, M> {
    pub fn host(self, host: impl Into<String>) -> ServerConfigBuilder<Set, P, M> {
        ServerConfigBuilder {
            host: Some(host.into()),
            port: self.port,
            max_connections: self.max_connections,
            tls_cert: self.tls_cert,
            read_timeout_ms: self.read_timeout_ms,
            log_level: self.log_level,
            _host: PhantomData,
            _port: PhantomData,
            _max_conn: PhantomData,
        }
    }
}

// Setting `port` transitions PortState from NotSet to Set
impl<H, M> ServerConfigBuilder<H, NotSet, M> {
    pub fn port(self, port: u16) -> ServerConfigBuilder<H, Set, M> {
        ServerConfigBuilder {
            host: self.host,
            port: Some(port),
            max_connections: self.max_connections,
            tls_cert: self.tls_cert,
            read_timeout_ms: self.read_timeout_ms,
            log_level: self.log_level,
            _host: PhantomData,
            _port: PhantomData,
            _max_conn: PhantomData,
        }
    }
}

// Setting `max_connections` transitions MaxConnState
impl<H, P> ServerConfigBuilder<H, P, NotSet> {
    pub fn max_connections(self, max: usize) -> ServerConfigBuilder<H, P, Set> {
        ServerConfigBuilder {
            host: self.host,
            port: self.port,
            max_connections: Some(max),
            tls_cert: self.tls_cert,
            read_timeout_ms: self.read_timeout_ms,
            log_level: self.log_level,
            _host: PhantomData,
            _port: PhantomData,
            _max_conn: PhantomData,
        }
    }
}

// Optional fields — available in ANY state
impl<H, P, M> ServerConfigBuilder<H, P, M> {
    pub fn tls_cert(mut self, cert: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.tls_cert = Some(cert.into());
        self
    }

    pub fn read_timeout_ms(mut self, timeout: u64) -> Self {
        self.read_timeout_ms = timeout;
        self
    }

    pub fn log_level(mut self, level: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.log_level = level.into();
        self
    }
}

// build() ONLY available when ALL required fields are Set
impl ServerConfigBuilder<Set, Set, Set> {
    pub fn build(self) -> ServerConfig {
        ServerConfig {
            host: self.host.unwrap(),  // Safe: typestate guarantees Some
            port: self.port.unwrap(),
            max_connections: self.max_connections.unwrap(),
            tls_cert: self.tls_cert,
            read_timeout_ms: self.read_timeout_ms,
            log_level: self.log_level,
        }
    }
}

// ============================================================
// Usage — compile-time enforcement
// ============================================================

fn main() {
    // This compiles — all required fields set:
    let config = ServerConfig::builder()
        .host("0.0.0.0")
        .port(8080)
        .max_connections(1000)
        .tls_cert("/path/to/cert.pem")
        .read_timeout_ms(5000)
        .build();

    println!("{:?}", config);

    // This would NOT compile — port not set:
    // let bad = ServerConfig::builder()
    //     .host("0.0.0.0")
    //     .max_connections(1000)
    //     .build();  // ERROR: build() not available for <Set, NotSet, Set>

    // Order doesn't matter:
    let config2 = ServerConfig::builder()
        .port(3000)
        .max_connections(500)
        .host("localhost")
        .build();
}
```

The typestate pattern makes missing required fields a compile-time error, not a runtime panic. `build()` is only implemented for `ServerConfigBuilder<Set, Set, Set>` — if any required field is `NotSet`, the method doesn't exist and the compiler reports it. Optional fields use `mut self` (same type returned), while required fields return a new type (state transition). Zero-sized PhantomData types mean this has zero runtime overhead — it's purely a compile-time check.

instruction: Implement a state machine in Rust using the type system to make invalid state transitions a compile error, with support for state-specific methods and data.
output:
<think>
State machines are a natural fit for Rust's type system. The idea: each state is a separate type, and transitions are methods that consume one state type and return another. Invalid transitions simply don't have method implementations.

Compare with the runtime approach (enum + match): it catches invalid transitions at runtime with panics or Result. The typestate approach catches them at compile time.

Design considerations:
1. Each state can carry different data — a `Draft` has edit history, a `Published` has publish date.
2. Transitions should consume the old state (move semantics prevents using a state after transition).
3. Some states share common functionality — use traits.
4. Guard conditions on transitions — return Result if a transition might fail.

I'll implement an order processing state machine: Draft -> Submitted -> Approved -> Shipped -> Delivered, with invalid transitions being compile errors.
</think>

```rust
use std::time::SystemTime;

// ============================================================
// State types — each carries state-specific data
// ============================================================

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Draft {
    items: Vec<String>,
    created_at: SystemTime,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Submitted {
    items: Vec<String>,
    submitted_at: SystemTime,
    customer_note: Option<String>,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Approved {
    items: Vec<String>,
    approved_by: String,
    approved_at: SystemTime,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Shipped {
    items: Vec<String>,
    tracking_number: String,
    shipped_at: SystemTime,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Delivered {
    items: Vec<String>,
    delivered_at: SystemTime,
    signature: String,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Cancelled {
    reason: String,
    cancelled_at: SystemTime,
}

// ============================================================
// Order<State> — generic over state
// ============================================================

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Order<State> {
    id: u64,
    state: State,
}

// ============================================================
// Shared behavior across all states
// ============================================================

impl<S> Order<S> {
    pub fn id(&self) -> u64 {
        self.id
    }
}

// Trait for states that have items
trait HasItems {
    fn items(&self) -> &[String];
}

impl HasItems for Draft { fn items(&self) -> &[String] { &self.items } }
impl HasItems for Submitted { fn items(&self) -> &[String] { &self.items } }
impl HasItems for Approved { fn items(&self) -> &[String] { &self.items } }
impl HasItems for Shipped { fn items(&self) -> &[String] { &self.items } }
impl HasItems for Delivered { fn items(&self) -> &[String] { &self.items } }

impl<S: HasItems> Order<S> {
    pub fn item_count(&self) -> usize {
        self.state.items().len()
    }
}

// ============================================================
// State-specific methods and transitions
// ============================================================

// --- Draft state ---
impl Order<Draft> {
    pub fn new(id: u64) -> Self {
        Order {
            id,
            state: Draft {
                items: Vec::new(),
                created_at: SystemTime::now(),
            },
        }
    }

    /// Only drafts can add items.
    pub fn add_item(&mut self, item: String) {
        self.state.items.push(item);
    }

    /// Transition: Draft -> Submitted (consumes Draft)
    pub fn submit(self, note: Option<String>) -> Result<Order<Submitted>, Order<Draft>> {
        if self.state.items.is_empty() {
            return Err(self); // Can't submit empty order
        }
        Ok(Order {
            id: self.id,
            state: Submitted {
                items: self.state.items,
                submitted_at: SystemTime::now(),
                customer_note: note,
            },
        })
    }

    /// Transition: Draft -> Cancelled
    pub fn cancel(self, reason: String) -> Order<Cancelled> {
        Order {
            id: self.id,
            state: Cancelled {
                reason,
                cancelled_at: SystemTime::now(),
            },
        }
    }
}

// --- Submitted state ---
impl Order<Submitted> {
    pub fn customer_note(&self) -> Option<&str> {
        self.state.customer_note.as_deref()
    }

    /// Transition: Submitted -> Approved
    pub fn approve(self, approver: String) -> Order<Approved> {
        Order {
            id: self.id,
            state: Approved {
                items: self.state.items,
                approved_by: approver,
                approved_at: SystemTime::now(),
            },
        }
    }

    /// Transition: Submitted -> Draft (rejection = back to draft)
    pub fn reject(self) -> Order<Draft> {
        Order {
            id: self.id,
            state: Draft {
                items: self.state.items,
                created_at: SystemTime::now(),
            },
        }
    }

    pub fn cancel(self, reason: String) -> Order<Cancelled> {
        Order {
            id: self.id,
            state: Cancelled { reason, cancelled_at: SystemTime::now() },
        }
    }
}

// --- Approved state ---
impl Order<Approved> {
    pub fn approved_by(&self) -> &str {
        &self.state.approved_by
    }

    /// Transition: Approved -> Shipped
    pub fn ship(self, tracking: String) -> Order<Shipped> {
        Order {
            id: self.id,
            state: Shipped {
                items: self.state.items,
                tracking_number: tracking,
                shipped_at: SystemTime::now(),
            },
        }
    }
}

// --- Shipped state ---
impl Order<Shipped> {
    pub fn tracking_number(&self) -> &str {
        &self.state.tracking_number
    }

    /// Transition: Shipped -> Delivered
    pub fn deliver(self, signature: String) -> Order<Delivered> {
        Order {
            id: self.id,
            state: Delivered {
                items: self.state.items,
                delivered_at: SystemTime::now(),
                signature,
            },
        }
    }
}

// --- Delivered state: terminal, no transitions ---
impl Order<Delivered> {
    pub fn signature(&self) -> &str {
        &self.state.signature
    }
}

// --- Cancelled state: terminal, no transitions ---
impl Order<Cancelled> {
    pub fn reason(&self) -> &str {
        &self.state.reason
    }
}

// ============================================================
// Usage
// ============================================================

fn main() {
    let mut order = Order::new(1001);
    order.add_item("Widget A".into());
    order.add_item("Widget B".into());

    // Draft -> Submitted
    let submitted = order.submit(Some("Rush delivery".into())).unwrap();
    
    // Submitted -> Approved
    let approved = submitted.approve("manager@co.com".into());
    println!("Approved by: {}", approved.approved_by());
    
    // Approved -> Shipped
    let shipped = approved.ship("TRACK-12345".into());
    println!("Tracking: {}", shipped.tracking_number());
    
    // Shipped -> Delivered
    let delivered = shipped.deliver("J. Smith".into());
    println!("Delivered! Signed by: {}", delivered.signature());

    // COMPILE ERROR: can't ship a Draft
    // let bad = Order::new(2).ship("TRACK".into());
    
    // COMPILE ERROR: can't add items to Shipped order
    // shipped.add_item("Extra Widget".into());
}
```

The typestate pattern encodes the order lifecycle in the type system: `Order<Draft>` has `add_item()` and `submit()`, but NOT `ship()`. `Order<Shipped>` has `deliver()` and `tracking_number()`, but NOT `add_item()`. Each transition method consumes `self` (move semantics), making it impossible to use a state after transitioning. The `submit()` returns `Result` for guard conditions (can't submit empty order) — if it fails, you get the `Order<Draft>` back to fix and retry.
